nodes.label:
Anchoring
Bias
nodes.title: First number sticks — early QC projections set expectations that persist despite contrary evidence
nodes.example: When a company announces '1,000 qubits by 2025,' that number anchors expectations even when later data shows those qubits lack error correction.

nodes.label:
Authority
Bias
nodes.title: Experts must be right — prestigious names lend credibility to unproven claims
nodes.example: Nobel laureates endorsing QC timelines carry outsized weight, even when their expertise is in theory, not engineering.

nodes.label:
Bandwagon
Effect
nodes.title: Everyone's investing — herd behavior drives investment independent of evidence
nodes.example: When Google, IBM, and Microsoft all invest billions, other firms follow regardless of independent technical assessment.

nodes.label:
Confirmation
Bias
nodes.title: See only supporting evidence — investors notice QC successes, ignore failures
nodes.example: Investors highlight quantum supremacy announcements but overlook that those demonstrations solved no commercially useful problem.

nodes.label:
Sunk Cost
Fallacy
nodes.title: Can't stop now — past investment justifies continued spending despite poor outlook
nodes.example: 'We've spent $2 billion already — we can't walk away now.' But past spending says nothing about future probability of success.

nodes.label:
Optimism
Bias
nodes.title: It'll work out — systematic overestimation of positive outcomes
nodes.example: QC roadmaps consistently predict breakthroughs 5-10 years out. The timeline never gets shorter.

nodes.label:
Dunning-
Kruger
nodes.title: Overestimate understanding — non-physicists underestimate QC's technical barriers
nodes.example: Tech executives who can't explain decoherence confidently predict when fault-tolerant QC will arrive.

nodes.label:
Availability
Heuristic
nodes.title: Vivid examples dominate — dramatic QC demos overshadow mundane failures
nodes.example: A single 'quantum advantage' headline is more memorable than hundreds of quiet lab failures, skewing risk perception.

edges.label:
anchors filter
evidence
edges.title: Anchored expectations cause investors to seek confirming evidence for the initial projection

edges.label:
prestige drives
adoption
edges.title: When authorities endorse QC, others follow their lead, creating bandwagon momentum

edges.label:
herd creates
sunk costs
edges.title: Bandwagon-driven investments create sunk costs that trap investors

edges.label:
filtered evidence
feeds optimism
edges.title: Seeing only confirming evidence sustains unrealistic optimism about QC timelines

edges.label:
sunk costs
filter evidence
edges.title: Having invested heavily motivates seeking evidence that validates the investment

edges.label:
optimism
sets anchors
edges.title: Optimistic projections become the anchoring numbers that persist in decision-making

edges.label:
overconfidence
defers to authority
edges.title: Those who overestimate their own understanding paradoxically defer to the wrong authorities

edges.label:
vivid successes
fuel optimism
edges.title: Memorable QC demonstrations create an illusion that breakthroughs are frequent and imminent

edges.label:
dramatic demos
drive bandwagon
edges.title: Vivid, widely-shared QC announcements attract more followers to the bandwagon

edges.label:
optimists seek
confirmation
edges.title: Optimistic expectations drive selective attention to supporting evidence

edges.label:
consensus
validates experts
edges.title: When everyone is investing, the authorities who endorsed QC appear vindicated

edges.label:
ignorance breeds
optimism
edges.title: Not understanding the physics makes the engineering challenges seem more tractable

edges.label:
anchored targets
trap investment
edges.title: Anchored milestones create benchmarks that justify continued spending to reach them
